entry.title: Re: Integration between S/4HANA Cloud and Integration Suite with SOAP Outbound service using Output
entry.content:
@Arnab_G 

SOAP outbound from S/4HANA Cloud to Integration Suite:

  • Define outbound SOAP service in S/4HANA Cloud

  • In Integration Suite: SOAP sender channel + endpoint URL + security (cert/WS-Security) + mapping

  • Add retries and dedup keys to avoid duplicates

  • Monitor Integration Suite runtime and S/4 logs for failures

SOAP is reliable but heavy. Use REST/OData or events when possible.

entry.title: CK11N Picking Negative Procurement Price from Info Record (No PO Involved)
entry.content:
While executing cost estimate using CK11N, the system is determining a negative procurement price for a material.

  • The system correctly identifies the Purchasing Info Record (no Purchase Order is being picked).
  • However, the calculated price is negative, although:
    • The Info Record shows a positive price in ME13.
    • There is no PO involved in the valuation.
  • Valuation Variant:
    • Strategy: L – Price from Purchasing Info Record
    • Sub-strategy: B – Purchase Order Price via Condition Table
  • latest PO is return PO

entry.title: Inconsistent Behavior of SAP Build Work Zone Fiori Apps on Mobile (Joule Work App & Browser Access)?
entry.content:
Introduction

In our recent implementation of the My Time Sheet application via SAP Build Work Zone, we conducted multiple testing sessions after enabling external (internet) access.

While the application performs well in controlled environments, we observed inconsistent and non-deterministic behavior across mobile devices, browsers, and the Joule Work app. In some cases, the application works seamlessly, while in others, it results in partial loading or blank screens.

This article summarizes our findings and highlights key architectural considerations for the SAP community.

Architecture Overview

To provide full context, the solution is implemented using a standard SAP architecture with the following components:

  • Authentication: Azure Active Directory (Azure AD)
  • Authorization & Trust: SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS)
  • Frontend Runtime: SAP Build Work Zone (Standard Edition)
  • Mobile Access: Joule Work App
  • Backend Connectivity: SAP Web Dispatcher with Principal Propagation
  • Application Type: HTML5 / SAPUI5 (Fiori-based)

End-to-End Flow (Simplified)

Mobile / Browser
      ↓
Azure AD (Authentication)
      ↓
IAS (Trust & Authorization)
      ↓
SAP Build Work Zone (HTML5 App)
      ↓
Destination Service
      ↓
Web Dispatcher (Principal Propagation)
      ↓
Backend (e.g., S/4HANA)

This architecture introduces multiple authentication hops, session dependencies, and runtime layers, which may contribute to instability in mobile scenarios.

Testing Scope

Testing was performed across:

  • iOS devices (work & personal)
  • Android devices (work & personal profiles)
  • Work and private laptops (Chrome & Edge)
  • Access methods:
    • Direct URL (browser)
    • Joule Work mobile application

Observed Behavior

1. iOS (iPhone)

Browser Access

  • ✅ Works in Microsoft Edge
  • ✅ Works in Google app
  • ❌ Not working in Chrome

Joule Work App

  • ❌ App opens but remains blank / non-functional

2. Android

Browser Access

  • ✅ Works consistently

Joule Work App

  • ✅ Initially works
  • ⚠️ Stops working after some time (session inconsistency)

3. Desktop (Laptops)

  • ✅ Works reliably in Chrome
  • ⚠️ In Microsoft Edge, tiles sometimes fail to load initially but work after refresh

Key Issues Identified

1. Inconsistent Behavior

  • Same application behaves differently:
    • Across devices (iOS vs Android)
    • Across browsers (Edge vs Chrome)
    • Across sessions (intermittent failures)

2. Blank Screen Issue

  • Observed in both:
    • Browser (after successful launch)
    • Joule Work app (application shell loads, but content is empty)

3. Strong Browser Dependency

  • Chrome on iOS consistently fails
  • Edge and Google app perform better

4. Mobile App Instability (Joule Work)

  • iOS: Not working
  • Android: Works intermittently

Architectural Observations

Based on the current setup, the issue appears to be architectural rather than purely configuration-related.

1. Multi-Layer Authentication Complexity

  • Authentication via Azure AD
  • Trust and session handling via IAS
  • Backend access via Principal Propagation (Web Dispatcher)

This creates:

  • Multiple token exchanges
  • Session dependencies across layers
  • Increased chances of session misalignment

2. Session Handling in Mobile Environments

  • SAP Build Work Zone relies on:
    • Browser-based sessions
    • Cookies and storage

Mobile platforms (especially iOS):

  • Restrict cookies (SameSite policies)
  • Isolate sessions in WebView containers

Result:

  • Session loss
  • Token expiration not handled gracefully
  • Blank screen scenarios

3. Joule Work App Limitations

  • Likely uses a WebView container
  • WebView environments:
    • Do not fully share authentication context with browser sessions

Particularly problematic in:

  • Multi-hop authentication (Azure AD → IAS → Work Zone)

4. Distributed Runtime Dependencies

Application flow includes:

  • Work Zone
  • HTML5 App Repository
  • Destination Service
  • Web Dispatcher
  • Backend system

👉 Any intermittent failure in this chain:

  • Results in incomplete UI rendering
  • No clear error feedback to user

5. iOS Platform Constraints

  • All browsers use WebKit
  • Strict enforcement of:
    • Cookie handling
    • Session persistence

 Leading to:

  • Chrome not working
  • Inconsistent app behavior

Conclusion

While the application performs well under stable conditions, the current setup shows:

  • ❌ Inconsistent behavior across devices and browsers
  • ❌ Limited reliability for mobile usage
  • ⚠️ High dependency on session handling across multiple layers

This makes the solution not fully stable for productive mobile usage at this stage.

Questions to the SAP Community

I would appreciate insights on the following:

  1. Is this behavior a known limitation when using:

    • Azure AD + IAS
    • Work Zone + Joule Work app
    • Principal Propagation via Web Dispatcher

  2. Is Joule Work app officially recommended for:

    • Work Zone HTML5/Fiori apps in such architectures?

  3. What is the recommended mobile strategy?

    • SAP Mobile Start?
    • SAP MDK-based applications?

  1. Are there best practices for:

    • Stabilizing session handling across Azure AD and IAS?
    • Avoiding blank screen issues in Work Zone apps?

  2. Has anyone implemented a similar architecture successfully with consistent mobile behavior?

Final Thought

This experience highlights an important architectural insight:

SAP Build Work Zone works reliably in browser-based environments, but mobile usage—especially with external authentication providers and multi-hop architectures—requires careful alignment with SAP’s recommended mobile strategy.

entry.title: We are experiencing an issue regarding batch classification validation in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public
entry.content:
Dear Support Team, 

We are experiencing an issue regarding batch classification validation in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition.  

Current Configuration: 

  1. We have created a characteristic and flagged it as a Required Entry. 
  1. This characteristic is assigned to a Batch Class (Class Type 023), where it is also marked as a Required Entry (Class Overwrite). 
  1. The Batch Class is assigned to the Material Master record.  

The Issue: 
When creating a Production Order (CO01) and generating a batch under the Goods Receipt tab, the standard Batch Classification dialog pop-up appears as expected. However, the system allows the user to completely bypass the mandatory characteristic entry. The user can close the pop-up or process past it without entering a value, and the system saves the production order. 

Since the characteristic is explicitly flagged as a Required Entry, the system should enforce a hard block (Error) rather than a by passable Information. 

We have also tested the scenario in CO11N. However, the system behavior remains the same. 

Our Questions: 

  1. Is there any standard configuration available in the Public Cloud edition to enforce a hard validation error for incomplete batch classification during order creation/release? 
  1. If this is standard behaviour at the order creation stage, could you please provide the exact technical name of the cloud-ready BAdI (Business Add-In) or Enhancement Spot available to validate batch classification data upon saving the production order? 

I have attached screenshots of our characteristic/class configuration and the order creation screen for your reference.

Regards,

Shubham

entry.title: TM Public Cloud - transportation scheduling without 46P
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

I have a question regarding SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Transportation Management.

In the Starter System SAP TM determines planned departure and arrival dates for a freight order, even though scope item 46P is not activated. The distance fields in the locations are empty, the route is also not maintained in the sales order.

How does SAP TM determine the transport duration in this case? What is the standard logic behind it?

If I understand correctly, the scope item 46P is not mandatory, it is needed for automatic distance and duration calculation based on geocoordinates. But what are the standard options if 46P is not used?

Thank you in advance.

entry.title: Locking Product Master Fields: Protecting Your Product Data
entry.content:
Managing Product Master Data Field Locking

Managing product master data requires careful control, especially when certain information needs to remain unchanged. SAP's field locking functionality provides a robust solution for protecting critical product data from unintended modifications. 

What Is Field Locking? 

Field locking allows you to designate specific fields in your product master records as "lock-relevant." Once a product master record is locked, these protected fields cannot be modified through the Manage Product Master Data app. This safeguard activates immediately when you save the record. 

 Getting Started 

 To use this feature, your business role needs the catalog SAP_CMD_BC_PR_DIGITAL_LOCK_PC. Alternatively, you can create a role using the template SAP_BR_PRODMASTER_SPECIALIST, which includes the necessary authorization. Your system administrator must also grant you permission to lock (and in exceptional cases, unlock) product master records. 

 Configuring Lock-Relevant Fields 

 SAP provides two configuration activities for defining which fields should be lock-relevant: 

Define Lock-Relevant Fields (OMSFIX): This activity works with existing material master fields for industry. You can enable or disable lock-relevance for these fields, but you cannot add new ones. In test systems, you have flexibility to change settings anytime. However, in production systems, you can only remove lock-relevance if no locked materials exist. 
To access the activity, use SPRO transaction and navigate to Logistics - General > Material Master > Field Selection > Define Lock Relevant Fields.

Define Lock-Relevant Fields in Product Master (OMSFIXC): This enhanced activity offers more flexibility—you can add new entries and enable lock-relevance for additional fields. Changes made here take precedence over OMSFIX settings, except for extensible fields. 

  • In SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition: To access the activity, use SPRO transaction and navigate to Logistics - General > Material Master > Field Selection > Define Lock Relevant Fields in Product Master.
  • In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: To access the configuration step, go to Database Management > Product > Define Lock-Relevant Fields in Product Master in your configuration environment.

Note: Both OMSFIXC and OMSFIX are available in SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. Only OMSFIXC is available in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. 

Working with Locked Products 

 Locking a product is straightforward: simply select the "Product is Locked" checkbox under Basic Data in the Manage Product Master Data app.  

 Need to make changes to a locked product? With proper authorization, you can temporarily unlock the record, make your edits, then re-lock it before saving. However, remember that once locked, products typically cannot be unlocked unless you have special authorization and the product isn't used in configuration management. 

 Important Considerations 

 Keep these points in mind when implementing field locking: 

  • By default, no fields are lock-relevant in the standard system—you must configure this yourself 
  • If you flag a field as lock-relevant after scheduling changes for that field in a locked record, those scheduled changes will be lost 
  • This functionality applies only to product master for industry, not retail 
  • The lock takes effect only after saving the record 

 Field locking is a powerful tool for maintaining data integrity in your product master records. By carefully selecting which fields to protect and establishing clear authorization protocols, you can prevent accidental changes while maintaining the flexibility your business needs. 

 Have you implemented field locking in your SAP environment? What challenges or benefits have you experienced? I'd love to hear about your experiences in the comments below.

entry.title: SAP BW Modernization and Stewardship with SAP Business Data Cloud - Introduction
entry.content:
Lift - Shift - Innovate - Govern - Decommission

This is the introduction blog of a series that covers the phases of the SAP BW modernization journey. 

For over two decades, SAP Business Warehouse (SAP BW) has served as the analytical backbone of enterprises, supporting financial reporting, operational intelligence, and regulatory compliance. Many continue to rely on SAP BW 7.5, SAP BW powered by SAP HANA, and SAP BW/4HANA to consolidate and govern mission-critical business data. 

However, the conditions under which SAP BW was originally designed have changed significantly. Enterprises now operate in a data environment characterized by larger volumes, faster decision cycles, broader integration requirements, and increased pressure to support advanced analytics and artificial intelligence. Business users no longer expect only structured historical reporting. They expect near-real-time insight, self-service access to trusted data, cross-domain semantic consistency, and support for predictive and AI-driven decision-making. At the same time, IT organizations are under constant pressure to reduce total cost of ownership, simplify operations, improve governance, and modernize aging data platforms before maintenance deadlines create forced decisions. 

These pressures are not independent. They reinforce each other. Rising infrastructure costs make it harder to justify on-premises investment. The shift to AI-ready architectures highlights the limitations of legacy modeling. The maintenance horizon places a hard upper limit on how long delay is feasible. Together, they make clear that modernization is no longer optional. The only question is how to execute it. 

This is the context in which SAP BW Modernization and Stewardship has become a strategic priority - not merely a technology upgrade, but a deliberate repositioning of the enterprise data platform for a new era. 

SAP Business Data Cloud: From Monolithic Data Warehouse to Business Data Fabric 

The architectural shift is significant. Traditional BW landscapes are centralized by design. They combine ingestion, transformation, persistence, semantic logic, and reporting in a tightly coupled model. That design brought control and consistency, but it also created bottlenecks. Over time, many enterprises found that custom logic, data duplication and silos expanded continuously, while analytical agility declined. 

SAP BDC introduces a much different architecture and philosophy. Enterprises are guided away from rigid, monolithic data warehouses toward a business data fabric and a decentralized data mesh. BW assets can be retained, exposed, modernized, governed, and eventually retired in a phased approach: Lift, Shift, Innovate, Govern, and Decommission, avoiding a high-risk big bang migration. The main phases are Lift (1), Shift (2) and Innovate (3) complemented by Govern (4) and finally Decommission (5). 

All phases form both a sequence of activities and the structure of this blog series. 

  1. Lift - Establish the foundation: Migrate SAP BW / BW4HANA on-premises to SAP BDC (SAP BW PCE) as-is to establish a stable foundation without disrupting current operations. 
  2. Shift - Begin the modernization journey: Publish SAP BW data as governed data products within SAP Business Data Cloud, making it reusable. 
  3. Innovate - Create an AI ready data platform: Adopt SAP-managed data products, intelligent applications, and cloud-native analytical patterns, replacing legacy SAP BW-centered use cases. 
  4. Govern - Ensure data integrity, compliance and user trust: Establish the stewardship controls for a sustainable data product operating model. 
  5. Decommission - Gradually retire SAP BW until it’s no longer required. 

These phases are not strictly linear. Different business domains may move through them at different speeds. Some may remain in coexistence for a while, while others modernize quickly. Governance spans the entire journey, and decommissioning should be planned early even if executed later. 

If you're interested to start the BW modernization journey follow this blog series and get your free assessment.

entry.title: Business Data Architecture: Defining Business Meaning
entry.content:
The problem: When Revenue means different things

In the previous post, we explored how business meaning fragments as organizations scale - whether that scale comes from multiple platforms, large development teams, or both.

Without clear definition, "Revenue" means different things to different teams. Finance reports €10M. Sales reports €10.2M for the same period. Both are pulling from legitimate data sources. Both calculations seem reasonable. But the numbers don't match - and nobody can explain why.

This isn't a data quality issue. It's a definition issue. The business never explicitly agreed on what "Revenue" actually means.

This post shows why this happens - and how to prevent it by separating what you measure from how you analyze it.

 

Why this happens: The report-centric pattern

This fragmentation isn't an accident. It's the predictable result of how some analytics environments evolve.

The pattern looks like this: You build a revenue report. It works. Business asks for another revenue report with different breakdowns. You copy the logic. Modify it slightly. Deploy it.

Six months later, "Revenue" means slightly different things in different places. One report includes returns, another doesn't. One deducts discounts, another shows gross. Finance's version uses invoiced amounts, Sales uses booked orders.

The business logic that defines "Revenue" is embedded in each report. There's no single place where "Revenue" is defined. Every report reimplements it.

When business requirements change - "We need to exclude cancelled orders from Revenue" - you have to find and fix every report, query, and model that calculates Revenue. And you'll miss some.

This is the report-centric approach: meaning lives in reports, reuse happens through copy/paste, and scaling means more divergence.

 

Three core concepts: Metrics, Measures, and Dimensions

Business Data Architecture prevents this by separating what you measure from how you analyze it.

In the report-centric approach, every report defines both at once. You build "Revenue vs Budget by Region" and the definition of Revenue, the Budget comparison, and the regional breakdown all live together in that one report. When someone needs a different view, they copy the logic and adjust it. That's where fragmentation starts.

Business Data Architecture breaks this apart into three explicit concepts. Each concept has a distinct responsibility, and they work together to prevent the copy/paste spiral:

If you're familiar with dimensional modeling, Measures and Dimensions aren't new - these have been standard practice since Kimball. What's different is the concept before: Metrics.

The separation between Metric and Measure is what prevents fragmentation. That separation requires the business to agree on something explicit before any analysis happens. Let's look at what that means for Revenue.

 

Metric: What the business wants to measure

"Revenue" is a metric - it represents a specific business outcome. It's defined once, independently of how you'll later analyze it.

Not "Revenue vs Budget." Not "Revenue YTD." Just "Revenue" - the monetary value from sales transactions, with explicit boundaries (what's included, what's excluded), owned by one domain (Finance).

What the business must agree on

For a metric to work as a stable foundation, the business must explicitly define three aspects:

  1. What business outcome does this represent? One clear statement. Not a list of use cases or scenarios - just what this metric fundamentally measures.
  2. What's included and what's excluded? Make the boundaries explicit. Does Revenue include returns? Are discounts already deducted? What counts and what doesn't? If you don't define this, different teams will make different assumptions.
  3. Who owns the definition? One domain - Finance, Sales, or Operations - is accountable for maintaining this metric. Not shared ownership. Not a committee. One domain owns it.

Example: How Finance defines Revenue

When Finance defines Revenue, they answer those three questions:

  1. It represents the monetary value actually generated from completed sales after deductions. That's the business outcome.
  2. It includes completed sales transactions - meaning orders that have been fulfilled and invoiced. It excludes cancelled orders. Discounts, returns, and allowances are already deducted. Those are the boundaries.
  3. Finance owns it. They're accountable for keeping this definition accurate as business processes evolve.

Notice what this definition deliberately avoids: it doesn't mention Actual vs Budget. It doesn't talk about trends or forecasts. It doesn't reference platforms or technical implementations. It just defines what Revenue fundamentally represents as a business outcome.

That's the foundation. The analytical views - Actual, Budget, Variance, Year-to-Date - come next, as measures.

 

Measure: How a metric is evaluated

A metric tells you what to measure. Measures tell you how to analyze it.

The same metric - like Revenue - can be viewed in multiple ways: Actual performance, budgeted targets, year-to-date trends, variance from plan. Each view serves a different analytical purpose.

Measures are the sanctioned analytical interpretations of a metric. They add context, comparison, and meaning - but they never redefine what the metric fundamentally represents.

Example: Revenue Measures
  • Revenue (Actual): Realized revenue from completed transactions → Result measure
  • Revenue (Budget): Planned revenue from annual budget → Performance indicator (KPI)
  • Revenue YTD: Cumulative year-to-date actual revenue → Trend analysis
  • Revenue Variance: Actual minus Budget → Explanatory context

Notice that each measure references the same "Revenue" metric. They interpret it differently - but they don't redefine it.

This separation matters because it creates stability. If "Revenue" always means the same thing - regardless of whether you're comparing it to budget, trending it over time, or breaking it down by region - then you can trust that every analysis starts from the same foundation.

 

Dimension: Business context for analysis

Dimensions provide the shared structure for analyzing metrics - Time, Organization, Product, Customer. When everyone uses the same fiscal calendar, the same organizational hierarchy, the same product categories, analyses align.

But dimensions only work when the underlying master and reference data has quality. Product categories with duplicates, organizational hierarchies with gaps, or inconsistent time periods make shared analysis impossible. Better dimension quality enables more detailed, reliable insights.

This becomes critical for self-service analytics and AI. When business users explore data or AI generates answers, they're navigating through these dimensional structures. Poor quality dimensions produce misleading analysis - regardless of whether a human or AI is doing the work.

 

Common concerns

The most immediate reaction is usually: "This sounds slower than just building reports."

It's true - the first metric takes longer when you define it properly. But the second, third, and tenth reports are faster because they reuse that definition instead of reimplementing it. More importantly: when requirements change, you fix it once - not in every report that uses it.

Another concern: "What if Finance and Sales can't agree on a common definition?"

The disagreement already exists - it's just implicit. That's why one reports €10M and the other €10.2M. Making it explicit doesn't create the problem; it surfaces it. And when genuine differences exist, separate metrics can coexist transparently: "Revenue (Finance View)" and "Revenue (Sales View)" rather than pretending they're the same thing.

Finally: "Isn't this only for greenfield implementations?"

No. You introduce it incrementally - one metric at a time. Extract the "Revenue" logic that's duplicated across five reports, define it once, and let those reports reference it. Every data platform starts with a first metric. This extends what already exists.

 

The takeaway

The report-centric approach works until it doesn't. One report becomes five. Five becomes twenty. Revenue quietly means different things in different places.

The separation changes this. You define "Revenue" once as a metric, then add measures for different analytical views. Everyone navigates using the same dimensional structure. When requirements change, you fix the definition once instead of hunting through twenty reports.

This isn't slower - it's faster at scale. The first metric takes discipline. The tenth metric proves the value.

Define what you measure. Separate how you analyze it. Build once, reuse everywhere.

 

Up next

In this post, we focused on what business meaning is and how to define it. The next post explores who owns that meaning as analytics scales:

Platform Responsibility in a Multi-Platform Analytics Landscape

  • How to make platform ownership explicit when analytics spans multiple systems
  • Ensuring metrics are defined once and shared safely - preventing duplication and drift

entry.title: Building Custom Joule Capabilities: Private Document Grounding with SAP AI Core
entry.content:
This is part of a series on building custom SAP Joule capabilities. Start with the initial blog post if you haven't set up your environment yet.
  • Blog Post 0: Getting started : Get started with Joule Capability Development using Joule Studio CLI
  • Blog Post 1: Joule Message Types: Overview of Joule message types with practical examples and recommendations
  • Blog Post 2: Document Grounding: Connecting Joule to document sources so capabilities can retrieve and cite content from your organization's files.
  • Blog Post 3: Private Document Grounding with SAP AI Core (This): Isolating grounding data to specific capabilities using grounding in SAP AI Core.
  • Blog Post 4: Role-Based Access Control: Restricting which users can invoke which scenarios and actions based on their IAS group membership and other IAS user attributes. 

Introduction

In Post 2 we shipped a Product Compliance capability that mixed live Northwind OData with documents indexed in Joule's shared grounding service. That setup works beautifully for content you are happy to expose to every Joule user. It stops working as soon as the documents need to stay scoped. Scoped to a single capability, scoped to a particular group of users, or simply confidential by nature.

Think salary bands. Bonus structures. Equity guidelines. Project bid documents only the sales team should reach. Internal guidelines that belong to one capability and nothing else. The kind of content that should only ever surface inside a controlled flow, for the right user, with no chance of leaking through a stray Joule prompt.

In this post we build exactly that. An HR Compensation Search capability backed by a private grounding pipeline running in your own SAP AI Core instance. The documents are indexed where you control them, retrieved through a BTP destination you own, and reachable only through the scenario you publish. Joule never sees the index. Other capabilities never see the index. Only your capability does.

By the end you will have:

  • A private grounding pipeline in SAP AI Core, indexing a Google Drive folder of HR documents.
  • A BTP destination that handles OAuth token retrieval so your capability YAML stays credential-free.
  • A working Joule scenario that retrieves and cites answers from the private pipeline.
  • The data-isolation half of a defence-in-depth model, ready to pair with the role-based scenario access layer from Post 4 (we will show what that addition looks like at the end of the scenario step).

Why Private Grounding?

Before building, it is worth understanding exactly what changes when you move from shared to private grounding.

With the shared service, your pipeline and its documents live in Joule's shared Document Grounding instance. Joule automatically searches all pipelines in that instance. Any user who asks Joule a question that semantically matches your documents will get an answer. Even if you gate your own custom capability with a visibility_condition, that gate only stops users from triggering your specific flow. It cannot stop Joule from surfacing the same content through its built-in grounding.

With AI Core and its orchestration workflow, your grounding pipeline lives in your own AI Core instance. By default, Joule has no access to it. The only way to reach the documents is through a system_alias that points at the AI Core instance, and that alias only exists in your custom capability. A user who bypasses this capability entirely gets nothing.

Here is the comparison at a glance:

AspectShared Grounding (Post 2)Private Grounding (Post 3)
System aliasJOULE_GLOBAL_DOC_GROUNDING (reserved)Custom alias, your BTP destination
AuthenticationHandled by Joule runtimeOAuth2ClientCredentials via AI Core service key
Document visibilityAll Joule usersOnly reachable through your capability
Pipeline managementJoule Document Grounding service AI Core Pipelines API
Extra header requiredNoneAI-Resource-Group

The rule of thumb is straightforward. Use shared grounding for company-wide knowledge bases and general content. Use private grounding for confidential content where data isolation is a requirement.

Architecture Overview

Here is how the full flow works with private grounding:

  1. An HR Manager asks Joule: "What are the salary bands for senior engineers?"
  2. Joule routes the query to the search_compensation dialog function.
  3. The function fires a POST request to the AI Core retrieval endpoint via the newly created AICORE_PRIVATE_GROUNDING BTP destination.
  4. The BTP destination service fetches a Bearer token from AI Core's OAuth endpoint and attaches it to the request.
  5. AI Core searches the private pipeline for semantically similar chunks.
  6. The top-ranked chunks come back in the response.
  7. Joule's LLM synthesizes an answer from those chunks and presents it to the user.

The key differences from the shared grounding service are steps 3 and 4. Instead of the Joule runtime routing through a reserved alias, a real BTP destination handles OAuth token acquisition. You configure this once in the BTP Cockpit. Your capability YAML never touches a credential.

Prerequisites

Before you begin, make sure you have:

  • Joule Studio CLI installed and authenticated (see Post 0)
  • SAP AI Core instance with the generative AI hub enabled in your BTP subaccount
  • AI Core service key with access to the document grounding APIs
  • Google Drive folder with compensation documents, shared with a GCP service account (see Setting Up Document Grounding with Google Drive on SAP BTP for the GCP service account setup)

Step 1: Set Up AI Core for Grounding

If you already use SAP AI Core for LLM orchestration, the document grounding APIs are available in the same instance. No separate service is required. If you do not have an AI Core instance, provision one in your BTP subaccount by finding "SAP AI Core" in the Service Marketplace and creating an instance with the extended plan (required for generative AI hub access).

Once your instance is ready, create a service binding. In the BTP Cockpit, navigate to your AI Core instance and open the Service Bindings tab. Click Create, give the binding a name, and confirm.

Once the binding is created, click View Credentials to open the JSON and note these four values:

Depending on your runtime environment, this may be a service key rather than a service binding. On Cloud Foundry, AI Core credentials are issued as a service key. On Kyma or other Kubernetes runtimes, they appear as a service binding. The JSON fields and values are identical in both cases, so the rest of this setup is unchanged.
JSON FieldPurpose
clientidOAuth2 client ID for the BTP destination
clientsecretOAuth2 client secret for the BTP destination
urlOAuth2 token endpoint base URL (append /oauth/token)
serviceurls.AI_API_URLBase URL for all AI Core API calls

Before making any AI Core API calls, fetch a Bearer token using the credentials from your service binding. Replace <url>, <clientid>, and <clientsecret> with the corresponding values from your binding credentials:

curl --request POST "<url>/oauth/token" \
  --header "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
  --data "grant_type=client_credentials" \
  --data "client_id=<clientid>" \
  --data "client_secret=<clientsecret>"

The response contains an access_token field. Copy that value and save it. You will need it for the next request and throughout the rest of this setup.

Create a resource group for grounding. AI Core organizes resources by resource group, and the grounding APIs require an AI-Resource-Group header on every request. A resource group used for document grounding must carry a specific label (ext.ai.sap.com/document-grounding: true). Without this label, the grounding service will not recognize the group. Create it with a POST request:

curl --request POST "$AI_API_URL/v2/admin/resourceGroups" \
  --header "Authorization: Bearer $ACCESS_TOKEN" \
  --header "Content-Type: application/json" \
  --data '{
    "resourceGroupId": "grounding",
    "labels": [
      {
        "key": "ext.ai.sap.com/document-grounding",
        "value": "true"
      }
    ]
  }'

You can choose any valid ID for resourceGroupId (3 to 253 characters, letters, numbers, . and - allowed, must start and end with a letter or number). Substitute it wherever this post shows grounding. If you have an existing resource group you want to reuse, send a PATCH request to the same endpoint with just the labels array to add the grounding label.

Step 2: Create the BTP Destination

This destination replaces the reserved shared grounding JOULE_GLOBAL_DOC_GROUNDING alias used in Post 2. The BTP destination service handles the OAuth token lifecycle. Your capability YAML simply references the destination name.

Open Connectivity > Destinations in your BTP Cockpit and create a new destination with these properties:

PropertyValue
NameAICORE_PRIVATE_GROUNDING
TypeHTTP
URL<AI_API_URL> from the service binding credentials
Proxy TypeInternet
AuthenticationOAuth2ClientCredentials
Client ID<clientid> from the service binding credentials
Client Secret<clientsecret> from the service binding credentials
Token Service URL<url>/oauth/token from the service binding credentials

Save the destination and click Check Connection. A successful response confirms BTP can reach the AI Core API with valid credentials.

This is the same pattern used for any OAuth2-protected SAP service. The destination abstracts authentication entirely. When your Joule capability sends a request through this destination, BTP automatically attaches a valid Bearer token.

Step 3: Configure the Google Drive Connector in AI Core

The following section covers the setup for document grounding with Google Drive. However, the same can be done via other allowed knowledge sourcs, such as Microsoft SharePoint.

AI Core's document grounding service uses generic secrets to hold connector credentials. Think of a generic secret as the AI Core equivalent of a BTP destination. It stores the connection details for a data source.

Create a generic secret holding your GCP service account key. The endpoint is /v2/admin/secrets and all credential values must be base64-encoded. You can base64-encode a value on the command line with echo -n "<value>" | base64. 

curl --request POST \
  --url "<AI_API_URL>/v2/admin/secrets" \
  --header "Authorization: Bearer <access_token>" \
  --header "AI-Resource-Group: grounding" \
  --header "Content-Type: application/json" \
  --data-raw '{
    "name": "gdrive-compensation",
    "data": {
      "clientId": "<BASE64_ENCODED_CLIENT_ID>",
      "private_key": "<BASE64_ENCODED_PRIVATE_KEY>",
      "client_email": "<BASE64_ENCODED_CLIENT_EMAIL>",
      "auth_uri": "<BASE64_ENCODED_AUTH_URI>",
      "authentication": "T0F1dGgyQ2xpZW50Q3JlZGVudGlhbHM=",
      "url": "aHR0cHM6Ly9nb29nbGVhcGlzLmNvbS9kcml2ZS92Mw==",
      "tokenServiceURL": "aHR0cHM6Ly9vYXV0aDIuZ29vZ2xlYXBpcy5jb20vdG9rZW4="
    },
    "labels": [
      {
        "key": "ext.ai.sap.com/document-grounding",
        "value": "true"
      },
      {
        "key": "ext.ai.sap.com/documentRepositoryType",
        "value": "GoogleDrive"
      }
    ]
  }'

The authentication, url, and tokenServiceURL values above are fixed base64-encoded constants. Do not change them. Only clientId, private_key, client_email, and auth_uri come from your GCP service account key JSON.

The name field (gdrive-compensation) is what you reference in the pipeline creation request. This is distinct from the BTP destination name.

Once the generic secret is created, create the pipeline pointing at your compensation documents folder:

curl \
  --request POST \
  --url "<AI_API_URL>/v2/lm/document-grounding/pipelines" \
  --header "Authorization: Bearer <access_token>" \
  --header "AI-Resource-Group: grounding" \
  --header "content-type: application/json" \
  --data '{
    "type": "GoogleDrive",
    "configuration": {
      "destination": "gdrive-compensation",
      "googleDrive": {
        "resourceType": "SHARED_FOLDER",
        "resourceId": "<your-compensation-folder-id>"
      }
    }
  }'

Note that configuration.destination refers to the generic secret name (gdrive-compensation), not the BTP destination name. The naming overlap is unfortunate but they are separate systems.

If you want to index only specific subfolders rather than the entire shared folder, add an includePaths array inside googleDrive. For example, "includePaths": ["CompensationDocs/2025"] would limit ingestion to that subfolder only.

"googleDrive": {
  "resourceType": "SHARED_FOLDER",
  "resourceId": "<your-compensation-folder-id>",
  "includePaths": [
    "CompensationDocs/2025"
  ]
}

The response includes a pipelineId. Save it. Poll the pipeline status until it returns FINISHED:

curl \
  --request GET \
  --url "<AI_API_URL>/v2/lm/document-grounding/pipelines/<pipelineId>" \
  --header "Authorization: Bearer <access_token>" \
  --header "AI-Resource-Group: grounding"

Ingestion for a small document set typically completes within a few minutes. For this example, upload the three sample compensation documents from the joule-dev-blog-samples GitHub repository under Blog_03_private_grounding/sample-documents/ to your Google Drive folder.

Step 4: Create the Capability

Create this folder structure inside your capabilities/ directory:

capabilities/
  private_grounding/
    capability.sapdas.yaml
    scenarios/
      search_compensation.yaml
    functions/
      search_compensation.yaml

Start with capability.sapdas.yaml:

schema_version: 3.28.0

metadata:
  namespace: joule.ext
  name: hr_compensation_search_capability
  version: 1.0.0
  display_name: HR Compensation Search
  description: Search confidential HR compensation policies grounded in private AI Core documents

system_aliases:
  AICORE_PRIVATE_GROUNDING:
    destination: AICORE_PRIVATE_GROUNDING

Unlike Post 2's JOULE_GLOBAL_DOC_GROUNDING, the destination name here (AICORE_PRIVATE_GROUNDING) is a real BTP destination you created in Step 2. The Joule runtime will look up this destination by name, retrieve credentials, and use them when making the API call. You own this destination, so you can update it, move it, or rotate credentials without touching the capability YAML.

Step 5: The Scenario

Create scenarios/search_compensation.yaml:

description: >
  Search compensation policies, salary bands, bonus structures, and equity guidelines.
  Retrieve grounded answers from confidential HR compensation documents.

slots:
  - name: query
    description: The compensation policy question to search for
target:
  name: search_compensation
  type: function

response_context:
  - value: $target_result.jouleResponse
    description: Joule answer to grounding query

The description covers the full range of compensation topics your documents address. Including concrete terms ("salary bands", "bonus structures", "equity guidelines") improves Joule's intent matching for this scenario. The response_context exposes the jouleResponse variable produced by the dialog function (defined in the next step) to Joule's LLM for response generation.

Optional: Restricting the scenario by user group

The capability above already isolates the data. Only this capability can reach the AI Core pipeline, and Joule information retrieval cannot. That is the data-isolation layer. You can add a second, complementary layer that controls which users are allowed to trigger the scenario in the first place. Joule supports this declaratively through a visibility_condition block on the scenario:

visibility_condition:
  objects:
    - type: ias_attributes
      attributes:
        - attribute: groups
          match: ANY
          values:
            - HR_MANAGERS

Adding this block to the scenario above gates it to users in the IAS group HR_MANAGERS. A user outside that group cannot trigger this scenario at all, even though they would otherwise be able to. Combined with the private pipeline, this gives you a defence-in-depth model. The data is reachable only through this capability, and this capability is reachable only by authorized users.

visibility_condition has its own dedicated post in this series that walks through the full attribute model (e.g. groups or email), the ANY vs ALL match modes, IAS group setup, and how to combine multiple condition objects. We will not configure it on the deployed capability in this post so that you can test the data-isolation layer in isolation.

Step 6: The Dialog Function

Create functions/search_compensation.yaml:

parameters:
  - name: query
    optional: false
action_groups:
  - actions:
      - type: api-request
        method: POST
        headers:
          content-type: application/json
          AI-Resource-Group: grounding
        system_alias: AICORE_PRIVATE_GROUNDING
        path: "/v2/lm/document-grounding/retrieval/search"
        body: >
          {
            "query": "<? query ?>",
            "filters": [
              {
                "id": "compensation-filter",
                "searchConfiguration": {
                  "maxChunkCount": 3
                },
                "dataRepositories": ["*"],
                "dataRepositoryType": "vector",
                "documentMetadata": [],
                "chunkMetadata": []
              }
            ]
          }
        result_variable: search_results
      - type: set-variables
        variables:
          - name: jouleResponse
            value: "The result for the given userQuery - <? query ?> is: <? search_results.body ?>"

result:
  jouleResponse: <? jouleResponse ?>

Comparing this to Post 2's function, three things changed:

system_alias: AICORE_PRIVATE_GROUNDING replaces JOULE_GLOBAL_DOC_GROUNDING. This tells the runtime to use your BTP destination instead of the reserved alias.

path: "/v2/lm/document-grounding/retrieval/search" is the full AI Core API path. With the shared grounding service, the path was /retrieval/api/v1/search because the alias already pointed at the grounding service base URL. Here the destination URL is the AI Core root, so the full path is required.

AI-Resource-Group: grounding is a header that AI Core requires on every document grounding API call. It scopes the request to the resource group you created in Step 1. If you used a different ID for your resource group, substitute it here.

The retrieval body, maxChunkCount: 3, and the jouleResponse pattern follow the same logic as Post 2. Fetch the top three chunks and let Joule's LLM pick the most relevant content for the user's question. The AI Core retrieval endpoint returns the same response structure as the shared grounding service.

Step 7: Deploy and Test

Validate and deploy:

joule lint
joule deploy -c -n "hr_compensation_test"
joule launch "hr_compensation_test"

Test the capability inside the hr_compensation_test test instance you launched above. Try these queries:

  • "What are the salary bands for senior engineers?"
  • "What is the bonus structure for IC3 level?"
  • "How does equity vesting work at ACME?"
  • "What are the RSU grant ranges for principal level?"

Now verify the isolation. Switch from the hr_compensation_test window to the central sap_digital_assistant Joule and ask the same question directly without going through your capability: "What are the salary bands at ACME?" You should get a response indicating no relevant documents were found. The compensation documents are not in the shared index, so central Joule cannot surface them. The capability is the only path to the data.

If the compensation search capability returns no results, check the resource group header. The AI-Resource-Group value in the function must match the resource group where you created the pipeline.

Comparing the Two Approaches

You now have two document grounding capabilities in your Digital Assistant:

  • Product Compliance (Post 2) Northwind product data combined with compliance guidelines from the shared grounding service, accessible to all employees through your capability and through Joule.
  • HR Compensation Search (this post): Confidential compensation data in a private AI Core pipeline, reachable only through this capability. Add a visibility_condition (covered in Post 4) to additionally restrict who can trigger the scenario.

Both capabilities coexist in the same Digital Assistant without conflict. The shared grounding alias and the AI Core destination are independent. Each capability handles its own retrieval path.

The decision between shared and private grounding comes down to one question. Should the information/document be available to all Joule users? If yes, shared grounding is simpler to set up and requires no BTP destination. If no, private grounding is the only option that provides actual data isolation.

A few additional guidelines:

  • Shared grounding works well for anything you would put on an internal wiki. Examples include office information, general HR policies, IT guides, and onboarding materials.
  • Private grounding is appropriate for any content where unauthorized access would be a compliance, legal, or business problem. Examples include compensation data, financial projections, M&A documents, legal analysis, and board materials.
  • You can run multiple private pipelines in the same AI Core instance and serve them from multiple capabilities. Each capability can independently add a visibility_condition once you have read Post 4: Role-Based Scenario Access.
  • If your data repository is Microsoft SharePoint, a third security option exists: per-document access control that mirrors SharePoint ACLs into the grounding index, so two users hitting the same scenario each see only the documents they are personally permitted to view in SharePoint. See "What This Post Does Not Cover" below.

What This Post Does Not Cover

The capability in this post uses the minimum surface of the AI Core grounding module needed to make the data-isolation point: one pipeline, one filter, three chunks, and Joule response generation. The same setup sits on top of a much larger grounding API that this post does not exercise. For a real-world HR or compliance corpus you will likely want some of the following.

  • Per-document metadata via a Metadata API Server. AI Core can call an OAuth2-protected metadata endpoint during pipeline ingestion to attach custom keys to each document, for example department, region, level, or domainId. Retrieval can then be scoped to documents for region=EMEA and level=IC5 rather than everything in the pipeline. See Prepare your Metadata API Server.
  • Multi-level metadata filtering and complex match expressions. The /retrieval/search payload accepts filters at the data-repository, document, and chunk levels, with ANY and ALL match modes and nested AND/OR expressions. This blog post sets all three filter arrays to empty. See Vector Search.
  • Post-processing and reranking. The retrieval endpoint accepts a postProcessing block that can merge results across multiple filters or reorder them using the Cohere 3.5 reranker, including metadata-based boosting. For three sample documents, raw vector similarity is enough. For a larger compensation corpus that mixes salary bands, bonus structures, and equity policies, reranking and boosting on the user's level or region make a measurable difference to result quality. See Retrieval Search.
  • Other ingestion paths. The same grounding module supports Microsoft SharePoint, AWS S3, SFTP, SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Document Management service, and ServiceNow alongside Google Drive. 
  • Per-document access control (Microsoft SharePoint only). When the data repository is Microsoft SharePoint, you can set accessControlEnabled: true on the pipeline. The grounding service then mirrors SharePoint's per-document permissions into the index, including Entra ID security groups, direct user assignments, and inherited folder permissions. At query time, document grounding checks the signed-in user's identity against those permissions and returns only the documents that user is allowed to see in SharePoint. This gives you a third, per-document layer that sits on top of the data-isolation layer in this post and the scenario-level visibility_condition in Post 4. It also requires admin consent for the Group.Read.All and User.Read.All Microsoft Graph permissions, the user's IAS email must match their Entra ID email, and the setting is one-way (it cannot be turned off once enabled). At the time of writing it is available only on AWS eu10 and Google Cloud eu30. See Enable Document Access Control for Microsoft SharePoint.
  • SAP AI Launchpad. Pipelines, generic secrets, collections, documents, and executions in your AI Core resource group can be inspected and managed in the SAP AI Launchpad UI. The curl flow in this post is the developer setup path. Once the capability is wired, the people maintaining the document corpus do not need to touch curl.

None of these require changes to the capability YAML. Only the retrieval payload and the data and metadata behind it get richer.

 

Note for SAP SuccessFactors customers: If your goal is specifically HR policy documents in SuccessFactors Joule, there is a dedicated Manage Document Grounding admin app in AI Services Administration that handles connector setup, metadata, and user attribute mapping through a UI. This post targets sensitive documents in the general case and uses an HR use case for illustration purposes, where you own the AI Core instance and need full control over isolation

Conclusion

Building Custom Joule Capabilities: Document Grounding  established document grounding access to the shared grounding instance . This post covered an option to use document grounding only in specific capabilities . By moving your pipeline into AI Core and backing the system alias with a real BTP destination, you control who can reach the documents.

The capability code barely changes. The real work is infrastructure. You need an AI Core service key, a BTP destination, a generic secret in AI Core, and a pipeline pointing at a Google Drive folder. Once those are in place, the data is reachable only through this capability. Layering a visibility_condition on top of that, as covered in Post 4 - Building Custom Joule Capabilities: Role-Based Scenario Access in Joule, gives you the genuine two-layer model, with data isolation at the storage layer and flow control at the scenario layer.

In the next post, we look at gating the scenario itself by user attributes using visibility_condition and IAS group membership.

entry.title: New person in SAP
entry.content:
Hello everyone!

I realize there have probably been a lot of questions like this, but I’d like an answer specific to my situation. I’d like to start working in SAP – I worked at a large manufacturing company as a key user – primarily in the QM module, but I also dabbled in MM and other modules. I had no previous SAP experience. I was chosen because the people assigned to this role left the company, and they chose me because “I’m a computer person” and I’m sure I can figure it out. However, I wasn’t a typical key user – I didn’t receive any training, no one really taught me anything, I had to dig around to learn anything. Therefore, I have little, if any, knowledge of SAP. I really like the system and think I’d like to build a future with it. I have access to the SAP system and the SAP PRESS book on the MM module, because I’d like to start practicing with that module.

 

Is it a good idea to learn from a book and study it practically along with the system, or do you recommend any other learning methods? I’m guessing it’s difficult to get into consulting positions without any experience, but is there any proven form of learning that would allow me to get promoted to junior? Maybe if not a consultant right away, then a key user or something similar?

 

Maybe I should start with a branch other than MM? Maybe it’s different now due to some system changes, etc. I’m asking because I’m not up to date with how this system works. I’d be very grateful for any tips and honest opinions on whether it’s possible to get into this world without any experience in development, etc.

entry.title: Re: Change log at production order level
entry.content:
Hello 

Check SAP Note 390635.

This note introduces Log Change/History data for production and process order into CDHDR and CDPOS. A sample code is provided by SAP (available in Note correction instruction) to place in exit: EXIT_SAPLCOZV_001, which is triggered while saving of prod order.

As per note:

  • Changes will be logged to CDHDR & CDPOS based on separate Change Object for Production orders, for example ‘ZZORDER’ due to limitation in SAP.
  • Can display the change documents using, for example, standard programs RSSCD100, RSSCD150, or RSSCD200, since log display from standard transactions of the order is not supported.

Best regards,
Caetano

entry.title: Re: DRC for France einvoicing B2G and B2B
entry.content: Hi Paul, The BC set is referenced in the configuration guide for France DRC implementation. Please check this link https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ERP/f2ef139b3fda4a09bbb90ebfb7850e7a/11d8867a10fa49f1a72dd7656eb48108.html. Hope this helps. Kind regards, Lieve

entry.title: Re: Hide Award Configuration Fields from Workflow Approval Page of Reward and Recognition
entry.content:
@deepaksajjan While I wait for feedback, can you test this ?

2. "Respect Permission" on the Workflow Can Restrict Field Visibility If the workflow is configured with Respect Permission = Yes, participants on the workflow (Approver, Contributor, CC Role steps) will only see fields they have RBP permissions for. This means you could configure the approver's RBP role to have No Access on specific fields, effectively hiding them from that role on the approval page. [2] By default, Respect Permission = No, meaning all workflow participants see all fields regardless of RBP. Once set to Yes, field-level RBP overrides are respected on the workflow details page. Important caveat: This setting does not retroactively affect already-triggered workflows — it only applies to newly created workflow instances after the change. [2]

entry.title: Unable to assign new GL account to custom movement type which is a copy of existing movement type
entry.content:
We have a business scenario where separate consumption account need to be determined if the material is issued to cost center for R&D purposes and regular production consumption. 

We created a custom movement type 901 which is a copy of movement type 201 and assign a account modifier ZBR. Please see attached screenshot for additional information.

while setting up automatic account determination, we do not see general modification "ZBR" in the drop down. Please see screenshot for additional information. 

We cannot create a separate valuation class as it means we have to create a separate material for the same material which is practically infeasible suggestion.

Is there any enhancement option available to change the account assignment or is this item on the roadmap?

entry.title: Re: Error in Manage Automatic Payments app
entry.content:
Dear @Zlizardo,

No. In Public Cloud there is no standard function in Manage Automatic Payments / Payments Processed to manually add documents or attachments directly to the payment run. The app is intended to schedule payment proposals or payments and to monitor the proposal/payment status For a processed run you can display and print the proposal and payment lists and download the payment media but the app provides no option to store attachments on the run itself

More details below:

SAP Help – Manage Automatic Payments

SAP Help – Payment Proposal Lists and Payment Lists

As a clean-core workaround, supporting documents should be stored on the related standard business object, for example on the supplier invoice or on the related journal entry, where attachment handling is supported in the standard process. If a payment-run-level document repository is required, the clean-core option is a side-by-side document register or workflow on SAP BTP, linked by payment run ID, company code, fiscal year and/or accounting document number, while keeping the Cloud core unchanged

More details below:

SAP Help – Supplier Invoice Apps Grouped by Basic Operations

SAP Help – Manage Journal Entries – New Version

SAP Help – Document Management

Where the business requirement is to attach documents directly to the payment run in Manage Automatic Payments this is not covered by the current standard app behavior. The correct approach is to submit an improvement request in SAP Customer Influence.

More details below:

SAP KBA 2963059 – How to Submit Feature Requests for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Summary:

The missing add document icon in Payments Processed is expected standard behavior. Attachments should be maintained on the related invoice or accounting document not directly on the payment run in Manage Automatic Payments

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Vibe-coding in ABAP via an MCP Server with OpenAI Codex
entry.content:
@HarshTR  Honestly, I don't do these things manually anymore. I just ask the AI to do it. So in this case I would tell Codex someting like: "Here is the URL of a GitHub repository which contains an MCP server that can be used to connect to SAP systems and edit objects there. I need you to install it on my VPS and configure it so that you can use it with my ABAP Trial 2023 system which runs in a Docker container on the same VPS. Please ask me for the info that you need to be able to do this".

It might not do it right on the first try (or it might - Codex today is much more advanced than the one I had available 4 months ago), but then you just give it the error message(s) that came up and eventually it figures it out and installs / configures everything correctly.

entry.title: Re: Is MDM actually worth it?
entry.content: Great point Corrie. AI Context = Transactional Data + Master Data. Equipping AI solely with the current and historical activity of the business seems to leave a grounding gap for AI. The article you reference makes me wonder if AI without solid master data collapses like a house of cards missing the center supports ... Or ... is the margin of error actually acceptable?

entry.title: Re: How do I grant a Datasphere Open SQL Schema user read...
entry.content:
No go. Security. No Tables can be directly read via Open SQL. 

Workaround: 

- create CLI script: Creates Exposed Views from Tables (naming convention will help to identify the tables properly). Python. 

- create DB Procedure: Get all Exposable Objects and Grant Select. SQL

- run each hour f.e. 

This way you fully automated the process.

entry.title: Smarter, More Flexible, More Powerful: SAP Analytics Cloud Composites Innovation Roundup
entry.content:
Composites are keeping up with the pace of innovation in SAP Analytics Cloud — and honestly, I think the last 18 months are proof of that. We've shipped meaningful capabilities across every single quarter: governance features that make composite libraries manageable at scale, scripting improvements that give developers real flexibility, and integrations that extend composites well beyond the boundaries of SAC itself. The momentum is real, and it's not slowing down.

I would like to walk you through the highlights from 2025 Q1 through 2026 Q2, so you can quickly see what's new and how it may change the way your team builds and consumes analytics content.

 2026 Q2  · Composite Versioning and Others

Version Control for Composite Artefacts

Perhaps the most anticipated governance feature for composite designers: version history. Designers can now manage up to 10 versions of a composite. The current version remains active in any consuming story, while previous versions can be opened in read-only mode for review — without touching anything live. If a past version needs to be restored, a backup of the current version is automatically created before the rollback takes effect.

All versions — current and historical — always reference the latest state of models and shared resources, and transports between tenants carry only the active version. This feature fundamentally changes how teams can iterate on composites in production environments.

Interface Properties in Composite Calculations

Dynamic analytics scenarios — think threshold-based visualizations or configurable reference lines — can now be built using calculations that reference composite interface properties, with no scripting required. Story designers set those values per composite properties so a single composite can support multiple scenarios through configuration alone. Development effort drops, reuse goes up.

Composites in Lite Viewer

Composites are now fully supported in Lite Viewer, enabling faster story loading for users  — making composite-based content more accessible across the organization.

Importing SAP-Delivered Composites from Intelligent Applications

Users can now import SAP-delivered composites from intelligent applications in SAP Business Data Cloud directly into their own stories. This means your team can build custom stories on top of SAP's vetted composite components, benefiting from ongoing SAP updates while retaining the freedom to extend and customize.

Deep Copy Support for Composites with Data Models

During installation of SAP-managed intelligent applications, composite widgets and data models are replicated across the package, enabling reuse across multiple stories from a single installation. 

 See it in Action:

 

2026 Q1  ·  Scripting Improvements and Designer Experience

Optional Arguments in Composite Interface Functions

Designers can now define optional arguments in composite interface functions, appended after mandatory ones. When a script function is auto-exposed, changes to its optional arguments are automatically synced with the interface function. When new optional arguments are added, existing consuming stories require no adjustments — backward compatibility is preserved automatically.

Overwriting Composites with the Same Technical ID

Composite designers can now save a modified composite back over the original technical ID, preserving identity in the system while evolving the content. Consuming stories must be re-opened and re-saved to pick up the change — a small but meaningful quality-of-life improvement.

Auto-Incorporation of Composite Data Sources

When a composite is inserted onto the story canvas, its associated data sources are now added automatically — limited to sources the user has access to and that haven't already been added. This eliminates the prior manual step and reduces the risk of designer error during story assembly.

Chart Colors and Patterns Carry Over from Composites

Chart colors and patterns defined within a composite are now preserved in consuming stories, removing a manual reconciliation step and ensuring that composite-level design intent is carried through end-to-end.

For details you can refer to:

What's New in SAP Analytics Cloud | Deep Dive with Product Experts | Q1 2026

2025 Q4  ·  Discoverability and Script Reuse

Where-Used List for Composites

A where-used list is now available in the file repository and at composite design time, showing which stories consume a given composite along with name, description, owner, creation date, and modification metadata. Columns are sortable and the list is searchable — making composite lifecycle management tractable at enterprise scale.

Automatic Exposure of Script Object Functions

Custom script object functions can now be automatically exposed as reusable interface functions, eliminating the need to manually copy scripts across stories when sharing logic between composites and consuming stories.

 For details you can refer to:

What's New in SAP Analytics Cloud | Deep Dive with Product Experts | Q4 2025

2025 Q3  ·  Scripting Depth and Design-Time Flexibility

Script Access for Composite Properties

Composite properties can now be read and assigned in the script editor and calculation editor, with errors and reference lists supported — bringing composite property scripting to parity with how other object properties are handled in the SAC scripting environment.

Page-Size Setting Improvements

Composite design-time page dimensions are now configurable across a wide range: width from 128px to 7680px, and height from 32px to 7680px. For composites that need to fit precisely into specific layout contexts, this granularity matters.

 For details you can refer to:

What's New in SAP Analytics Cloud | Deep Dive with Product Experts | Q3 2025

2025 Q2  ·  Visual Fidelity and UX Alignment

Threshold Support Within Composite Definitions

Visual thresholds can now be defined and embedded within the composite itself, operating independently from story-level thresholds — no risk of cross-composite interference. The initial scope covers chart widgets with measure-based thresholds; table and dimension-based assigned color support is planned for subsequent releases.

Fixed vs. Dynamic Composite Size

A new fixed sizing mode joins the existing dynamic default, giving designers pixel-precise control over composite dimensions for layout-critical scenarios where consistency across different story canvases is required.

Unified Styling and Builder Panel Experience

The composite properties panel has been integrated into the Styling panel, and composite model configuration has been moved into the Builder panel — aligning composites with the interaction pattern used for charts and tables, and reducing cognitive overhead for story designers who work across widget types.

 For details you can refer to:

What's New in SAP Analytics Cloud | Deep Dive with Product Experts | Q2 2025

2025 Q1  ·  Foundational Capability Expansion

Global Calculations in Composite Widgets

Account, dimension, and cross-calculations can now be defined directly within composite widgets. Custom widgets with applicable calculation support are also covered. This closes a significant capability gap that had limited how analytically rich a self-contained composite could be.

Flow Layout Panel and Slider Widgets

The composite widget palette now includes Flow Layout Panel, Slider, and Range Slider. Flow Layout Panel enables composites that adapt gracefully to different screen sizes. Sliders enable interactive, dynamic composites — for instance, what-if simulation scenarios where users adjust inputs and see results update in real time.

Linked Analysis Support for Composite Target Widgets

Composites can now participate in linked analysis as target widgets. When a source widget drives a filter or selection, composite widgets — and the charts, tables, custom widgets, and text elements within them that share the same data source — will respond accordingly.

Option to Disable Model Configuration in Composites

A new Global Settings option lets composite owners lock down data model configuration, preventing story designers from substituting models when consistency must be enforced. It's unchecked by default, preserving existing flexibility.

 For details you can refer to:

What's New in SAP Analytics Cloud | Deep Dive with Product Experts | Q1 2025

Summary and Outlook

Stepping back across these 6 quarters of release notes, three clear themes emerge:

  • Governance and lifecycle management — Versioning, where-used lists, transport controls, and deep copy support are progressively turning composites from ad hoc reuse artifacts into properly governed, enterprise-grade content components.
  • Developer and designer experience — Optional arguments, auto-exposure of script functions, auto-incorporation of data sources, and unified panel UX are all reducing friction and aligning composites with the quality-of-life expectations of modern analytics developers.
  • Integration with the broader SAP ecosystem — Lite Viewer support, SAP Business Data Cloud intelligent application imports, and Datasphere model integration are broadening composites capabilities across the entire SAP Business Data Cloud, with full support for Datasphere models and an expanding set of intelligent applications

For analytics teams building on SAP Analytics Cloud, these innovations collectively lower the cost of building reusable content, raise the ceiling of what composites can express, and provide the governance infrastructure needed to manage composite libraries at enterprise scale.

We are continuously advancing composite capabilities through ongoing innovation. Please stay tuned for further updates !

entry.title: One Batch. Zero Guesswork. That's SAP Digital Manufacturing for Process Industries.
entry.content:
Introduction

Process manufacturing is unforgiving. A batch of carbonated beverage doesn't wait for the right information to arrive. It either runs right, on spec, on time, fully traceable, or it doesn't. And when it doesn't, the cost shows up everywhere: in scrap, in delayed orders, in quality holds, and in the hours spent reconstructing what actually happened on the shop floor.

Most plants are still managing that complexity with a combination of ERP systems that don't reach the floor, paper-based work instructions, manual quality logs, and OEE reports that arrive a day too late to act on. It works until it doesn't.

SAP Digital Manufacturing is built for exactly this environment. Here's what a single production shift looks like when everything is connected.

Everything Connected

It starts at 6 AM on the shop floor. A thousand liters of carbonated beverage need to be mixed, bottled, quality-checked, and shipped today.

The order arrives on the shop floor the moment it's released. When the demand planner creates a process order in SAP S/4HANA, it flows instantly into SAP Digital Manufacturing, with the recipe, the batch, the schedule, and an automatically-created inspection lot already attached. The production supervisor releases it in a single click.

No emails. No manual handoff. No gap between plan and execution.

The operator always knows exactly what to do. Helena, the production operator, opens her Mixing POD and sees her assigned orders, the phases to execute, and the work instructions including cleaning procedures and process diagrams, embedded directly in the screen.

She selects the right raw material batch, records process parameters, and the system validates every value against tolerance limits in real time. If a primary ingredient is short, it surfaces the approved alternates automatically. Nothing is left to memory or improvisation.

Quality is confirmed in the process, not at the end.

As Helena completes the mixing phase, she records CO2 content, temperature, water purity, and Brix directly against the inspection lot, right in the POD, with no separate system to open.

Each value is checked against spec instantly. By the time the batch moves to bottling, the quality record is already built. The quality manager sees a summarized valuation, all characteristics in range, and makes the usage decision in seconds, not hours.

When something goes wrong, the right people know immediately. During bottling phase, a machine goes down. The operator logs the downtime with reason code and duration directly in the Bottling POD and raises an alert to the supervisor with a single tap.

Meanwhile, the Line Monitor shows the plant manager in real time that availability has dropped, what the top scrap reason codes are, and where OEE losses are concentrated. Not in tomorrow's report. Now.

When the batch is done, everything is already traceable.

SAP Digital Manufacturing writes every confirmation, every goods movement, and every quality result back to S/4HANA automatically.

The Product Genealogy Report is available on demand: which sugar batch went in, what the phosphoric acid quantity was, what Brix value was recorded, when the finished goods batch was receipted. If a recall question arrives, the answer takes seconds, not days.

That's a full shift, plan to fulfilled product, with no paper, no manual reconciliation, and no guesswork.

Conclusion

For process industry manufacturers, this kind of end-to-end connection between planning, execution, quality, and traceability is what separates plants that react from plants that perform. It's not about replacing people on the shop floor. It's about giving every person, the demand planner, the production supervisor, the operator, the quality manager, the plant manager, exactly the information they need, exactly when they need it, with no friction in between.

The result is faster, cleaner production. Fewer quality holds. OEE data that drives real decisions. And a batch genealogy that's always ready for auditors, for regulators, and for customers who need to know their product was made right.

That's what SAP Digital Manufacturing delivers for process industries. And it starts with one batch, running right.

 

Do you like this post? Please let me know in the comments section what you think. Any feedback is highly appreciated.
Or, if you have any questions, please check SAP Community Q&A Area, or comment down below.

Thanks,
Manoel Costa

entry.title: Re: Hide Award Configuration Fields from Workflow Approval Page of Reward and Recognition
entry.content: @xavierlegarrec – Thank you very much for reviewing the question and providing a quick suggestion. I tested the option in the workflow to set “Respect Permission = Yes” at each workflow level. While this works well for most standard workflows, it does not fully fit the Spot Awards scenario. In Spot Awards, we have the following role requirements: Nominee (Employee Role): Needs access to Spot Award and Program for viewing Nominator Role: Needs access to Spot Award, Budget, and Program to view for their subordinates Because permissions are granted at the MDF level, the approver is often either a Nominee or Nominator, which makes the permission behavior complex. Additionally, our workflow includes: Manager’s Manager HR Manager In many cases: The initiator and final approver are the same person, so they must have access to initiate (and therefore can view the workflow) The Manager’s Manager level may also overlap with existing roles For the HRBP role, this setup might work; however, if the HRBP is also acting as a Nominator, they will still have visibility Because of these overlapping roles and MDF-based permissions, the “Respect Permission” setting does not fully restrict visibility as expected in this scenario.
